student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Say That Again -- The Commissioning Call at Full Speed
lesson.menu_desc: Uma diretora de projeto britanica que nao desacelera e um cronograma de comissionamento em velocidade nativa: future perfect, future continuous e as tres frases que param a call sem perder a cara -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 9 -- Say That Again
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Aula 9 -- Say That Again

listenings.text: João, it is Fiona, I have got two minutes between meetings so I will be quick. Two things, and one of them you will not like. The board asked me this morning, after our call, to bring provisional acceptance forward. So it is no longer the thirtieth of September. It is the twenty-third, and I am telling you now rather than letting you read it in a document next week. Nothing else moves. Site readiness on the twelfth, the integration test from the fifteenth, the dry run of the failover the week after, and the ramp-up in November, which was never negotiable anyway. Second thing, and this one is mine, not yours. The interface document from the rolling stock supplier is three weeks late and it is my side that is late, not yours. I am not going to pretend otherwise and I am not going to ask you to absorb it quietly. What I am asking is that you hold the test date, and that you tell me the moment it starts to hurt, not three weeks after it has started hurting. Send me the three-week look-ahead tonight. And João, please, do not build me a schedule that carries a workaround into provisional acceptance, and do not give me a date that has not factored in the night-shift restrictions on that site. I would rather have a date I can trust than a date I like.

Three rules, before your next international call, because the engineers who lose these calls are almost never the ones with the weakest English. First rule. The fast speaker is not testing you. She is not thinking about your English at all. She is late, she has three projects and a board meeting, and she talks that way to the man sitting next to her, who is English. Speed is not an exam. It is her Tuesday. Once you stop hearing it as a judgment, you stop freezing, and the moment you stop freezing you start catching about a third more than you did. Second rule. Stop hunting for the auxiliary. You were taught will have and will be, and in real speech they are simply not there. Will have collapses into something like willuv, we will have becomes we'll've, and will be almost disappears into the verb behind it. But the tense does not disappear with them, because it survives at the end of the phrase, in the participle. Closed. Energized. Running. Phased out. Listen to the last word, not the first, and the grammar comes back. Third rule, and this one is worth money. Never agree to a date you have not repeated back out loud. Not a nod, not an of course, not a yes yes. Say it back. Just to confirm, the twelfth, not the fifteenth. Could you run that by me one more time. It takes five seconds, it makes you sound like a professional rather than a nervous one, and I have watched a project lose a crew, a night shift and a whole test window because a very good engineer was too polite to spend those five seconds.

patterns.title: Four patterns that forecast a project
cards: The tense of the commitment. It says the work is finished BEFORE that point, not on it. This is the sentence somebody can hold you to, and it is the one the contract reads. "By the twelfth we will have confirmed site readiness in writing."

Not finished, not starting: happening, while the world moves around it. It is how you tell somebody what the site will look like on a given day. "This time next week we will be running a dry run of the failover procedure."
HOW LONG it will have gone on
It measures the effort accumulated up to that point, and it is how a cost or an extension gets justified. "By September the crews will have been working nights for six weeks."
The frame that carries the tense

BY is the deadline; UNTIL is the stretch. And after by the time, before, when, as soon as, the future is written in the PRESENT. "By the time the ramp-up starts, the workarounds will have been phased out."

patterns.text: "We will finish before the test" is a hope, and everybody in the call knows it. "By the twelfth we will have confirmed site readiness" is a date with a name on it. Same fact, same crew, same site: only the tense changed, and only one of them can be enforced.

items.situation: "Right, where are we on site readiness? By the twelfth I need that power supply energized." Give the status in one sentence, with the date in front.
tips: By the twelfth we will have energized the power supply and confirmed site readiness in writing.
And we will be working nights from the eighth to hold that date.

items.situation: She has said the acceptance dates in one breath and you caught only half. Stop her without apologizing.
tips: Could you run the acceptance dates by me one more time? I want to factor them into the look-ahead schedule.
I want to make sure I am following: provisional acceptance at the end of Q3, final acceptance twelve months later.

items.situation: Somewhere in a long sentence she moved provisional acceptance to the twenty-third. Catch it and confirm it out loud.
tips: Just to confirm — are you saying that provisional acceptance has moved to the twenty-third?
Then by the twenty-third we will have cleared every outstanding item on that list.

items.situation: "Can you still hold the integration test date, with the interface document three weeks late?" Answer without lying and without giving it away.
tips: We will hold the fifteenth. What I cannot hold is the fifteenth AND the two open actions on the cable route with the same crew.
I need to factor in the night-shift restrictions before I commit to anything beyond that.

items.situation: "Let us just keep the manual workarounds until final acceptance, to be safe." Refuse it.
tips: The workarounds get phased out before provisional acceptance, not after.
A temporary fix with no end date quietly becomes the design, and the operator pays to maintain it for thirty years.

items.situation: The call is ending in ninety seconds. Close it so that nothing is left to memory.
tips: Before we close: by the twelfth we will have confirmed site readiness, and by the twenty-third every outstanding item will have been cleared.
You will have the three-week look-ahead schedule tonight, and I will flag the interface risk the moment it starts to hurt.

bank.label: Committing to a deadline (will have + participle)
items: By the twelfth we will have confirmed site readiness in writing.
By the time we speak on Friday, we will have closed the two open actions on the cable route.
By the twenty-third every outstanding item will have been cleared, and the workarounds will have been phased out.

bank.label: Saying what will be in progress (will be + -ing)
items: This time next week we will be running a dry run of the failover procedure.
We will be working nights from the eighth to hold that date.
From the fifteenth we will be running the integration test between the interlocking and the control center.

bank.label: Stopping a fast speaker (and keeping your face)
items: Could you run that by me one more time?
Just to confirm — are you saying that provisional acceptance has moved to the twenty-third?
I want to make sure I am following: the deadline you are referring to is the twelfth, not the fifteenth.

bank.label: Refusing without closing the door
items: I need to factor in the night-shift restrictions before I commit to that date.
I will hold the test date. What I will not do is carry a workaround into provisional acceptance.
You will have a date tonight, and it will be a date I can defend rather than one I would like.
